deals.headline: Prism (Oyo parent) files UDRHP-I for ₹6,650 cr fresh-issue IPO
deals.significance: Oyo's holding entity files updated DRHP after prior approval, signalling an imminent roadshow for one of the marquee new-economy IPOs of H2 2026; entirely a fresh issue so dilution is equity-only, no secondary OFS overhang.
deals.pitch_angle: BRLM mandates on the Oyo/Prism book are live — engage management on institutional allocation strategy and anchor tranche; the all-fresh-issue structure gives BFSI-lending creditors no route out, keeping the equity story clean.

deals.headline: Aerion Energy Solutions board approves ₹10,000 cr QIP fundraise
deals.significance: A ₹10,000 cr QIP is one of the largest capital-raise mandates in the pipeline this week; institutional book-building window will be tight given market holiday on July 4 (US) and Saturday trade closure.
deals.pitch_angle: Approach Aerion's CFO immediately on BRLM selection; a QIP of this size will require at least two bookrunners and a domestic anchor allocation plan — an ECM desk with strong FII relationships has an edge.

deals.headline: Jio Platforms DRHP filed with SEBI for ₹35,000 cr IPO
deals.significance: Jio Platforms' DRHP is the single largest IPO filing in the current cycle; its listing will reset benchmark valuations for Indian digital and telecom-adjacent BFSI platforms.
deals.pitch_angle: Any bank not already on the Jio syndicate should target co-manager or selling-member roles; the deal will drive record institutional allocations and secondary block activity in Reliance Industries shares post-listing.

deals.headline: Airtel Money launches NBFC ops; digital lending mandate now live
deals.significance: Airtel Money's shift from payments to non-deposit NBFC opens a capital-raising cycle — the entity will need equity or subordinated debt to meet RBI's net-owned funds requirements as it scales lending.
deals.pitch_angle: Flag Airtel Money as a near-term ECM/DCM origination target; first capital raise as a regulated NBFC (equity or NCD) likely within 12–18 months, with Bharti Airtel as potential cornerstone investor.

deals.headline: CCI approves Aditya Birla–Times–Blackstone ₹16,700 cr RCB acquisition
deals.significance: CCI clearance of this all-cash ₹16,700 cr deal (acquirers: Aditya Birla Group, Times of India Group, Bolt Ventures, Blackstone) signals that large PE-backed consortium M&A is back in vogue; BCCI ratification is the remaining condition.
deals.pitch_angle: Track whether Blackstone seeks to monetise its equity stake via a PIPE or future IPO of the franchise vehicle; consortium structure also sets precedent for multi-investor sports-asset deals that may need dedicated ECM structuring.

market.headline: Nifty posts third consecutive gain, closes at 24,271; VIX at 11.80
market.significance: Three-session Sensex rally of 1.68% and Nifty of 1.69%, with VIX compressing to 11.80, reflects improving risk appetite — a supportive backdrop for primary market launches.

market.headline: Realty index surges 2.19%; IT and pharma also outperform on July 3
market.significance: Sector rotation into rate-sensitive realty and defensives (IT, pharma) while PSU banks, media and auto lagged — signals market is pricing in further monetary easing ahead of the next RBI meeting.

market.headline: US June nonfarm payrolls miss lifts Dow to record; Nasdaq lags on semis
market.significance: A weaker US jobs print increases Fed rate-cut probability, which softens the US dollar and US yields — a positive carry-through for Indian equities and FII flows on Monday open.

market.headline: FIIs net sellers ₹312 cr cash on July 3; DIIs absorb with ₹1,784 cr buy
market.significance: FII selling on July 3 snapped a three-day buying streak (cumulative +₹4,800 cr); DII bid more than fully offset it, limiting downside — dual-institution dynamic still net supportive for primary-market demand.

market.headline: India sat out global H1 2026 equity bull run; catch-up trade debated
market.significance: Indian indices underperformed global peers in H1 2026 following the West Asia shock; with crude now stabilising near $72/bbl and FII flows recovering, a catch-up rally is the key debate heading into H2 — directly affects IPO pricing ambition.

macro.headline: India 10-year G-sec yield eases to 6.72–6.73% on $3 bn foreign inflows
macro.significance: Foreign inflows via the Fully Accessible Route drove a 30 bps fall in yields over the past month to 6.72–6.73%; a lower risk-free rate compresses corporate bond spreads and improves QIP/NCD economics.

macro.headline: RBI holds repo at 5.25% (neutral); CPI at 3.93% in May, below target
macro.significance: Repo on hold at 5.25% with a neutral stance; inflation well below the 4% target gives the RBI headroom to ease — next policy meeting could pivot dovish, which is structurally bullish for equity valuations and ECM pricing.

macro.headline: Brent crude stable near $71–72/bbl; easing West Asia tensions key driver
macro.significance: Crude at $72/bbl (down sharply from West Asia crisis highs) reduces India's import bill, eases CAD fears, and limits RBI FX intervention pressure — directly improves the macro backdrop for capital-raising.

macro.headline: Goldman Sachs raises India 2026 GDP forecast 30 bps to 6.8%; projects $10 bn bond inflows
macro.significance: Goldman's upward revision, combined with a lower inflation and CAD forecast, validates the India re-rating narrative and supports FII re-engagement — key talking point on any international ECM roadshow.

macro.headline: US 10-year Treasury yields edge down to ~4.37%; weak payrolls amplify Fed cut bets
macro.significance: Softer US yields narrow the India-US rate differential in favour of Indian assets, reducing FPI outflow risk and supporting INR stability — a constructive backdrop for cross-border ECM marketing.

regulatory.headline: SEBI forms expert committee to review debenture trustee regulations
regulatory.significance: A SEBI review of debenture trustee rules could tighten lender-of-last-resort protections on NCD issuances — directly affects BFSI issuers who rely on listed debt as a capital-raising tool.
regulatory.pitch_angle: Alert NBFC and HFC clients that stricter DT norms may raise compliance costs on NCD issuances; window to close large NCD deals under current framework may be narrow — accelerate any pending debt-capital mandates.

regulatory.headline: SEBI proposes changes to AIF consent rules to strengthen investor protection
regulatory.significance: Proposed AIF consent-rule changes (likely requiring unitholders' affirmative vote on material portfolio decisions) would affect PE/VC funds — ChrysCapital, Kedaara, Multiples, True North could face higher governance requirements.
regulatory.pitch_angle: Engage PE/VC LP clients on implications for fund documentation and exit timelines; if consent thresholds rise, PE sponsors may accelerate secondary stake sales or IPO exits before rules finalise.

regulatory.headline: PFRDA launches AI-powered 'Pension Sahayak' grievance portal
regulatory.significance: While operationally focused, the AI-driven portal signals PFRDA's intent to deepen NPS penetration — larger subscriber base expands pension fund AUM, which in turn increases DII capacity to anchor large IPOs and QIPs.
regulatory.pitch_angle: Use as a conversation-starter with pension fund CIOs on anchor allocation capacity for H2 2026 large-caps; a growing NPS AUM base is a structural tailwind for domestic primary market demand.

regulatory.headline: RBI's June concessional FCNR(B)/ECB swap facilities spurred ₹279 bn FPI bond buying
regulatory.significance: RBI's June 5 liquidity-support measures directly catalysed ₹279 bn of foreign government-bond purchases and are now lifting equity risk appetite — the macro transmission from June RBI action is still playing out.
regulatory.pitch_angle: Highlight the RBI's capital-inflow facilitation measures in FII-facing ECM pitch materials as evidence of policy support for India re-rating — strengthens the case for anchor allocations from global EM funds.

pulse.one_line_read: Indian ECM enters the July 4 weekend on a constructive note — Nifty's three-day 1.7% rally, cooling bond yields at 6.72%, and a busy IPO pipeline headlined by Jio and Oyo/Prism set up a busy primary-market H2, even as FIIs remain cautious day-to-day.

card.title: The 'Why Should We Hire From a Domestic Franchise?' Question
card.question: A foreign bank MD leans back and says: 'You've only worked at SBI Cap. Our deals are cross-border, our clients are global institutions, and our bar is different. Why are you the right hire?' How do you think through and answer this — without sounding defensive?
card.teaching:
What the interviewer is actually probing: They are not asking you to apologize for SBI Cap. They want to see whether you have self-awareness, whether you can position a genuine differentiator, and whether you'll crumble under mild pressure. The trap is going defensive — 'SBI Cap is actually a great platform' — or overclaiming — 'I've done everything you've done.' Both lose.

The right reasoning pattern has three moves.

First, own the difference without apologizing for it. Domestic franchise, government-linked parentage, predominantly public-sector issuer base — say it plainly. Interviewers respect candidates who don't need flattery to feel secure.

Second, convert the 'limitation' into a genuine edge. You have sat across the table on deals where the issuer is also a quasi-regulator or a systemic institution — Bajaj Housing Finance (NBFC under RBI stress-testing scrutiny), NSE (governance complexity as a market infrastructure institution), NSDL (same layer of regulatory sensitivity). Foreign banks rarely let junior bankers near that issuer type with real access. You have seen how domestic anchor demand gets built, how HNI tranches behave in volatile windows, and how retail subscription dynamics in India differ entirely from any roadshow model a foreign bank uses for a Singapore or Hong Kong listing.

Third, name what you're coming to learn, not just what you're bringing. Say something like: 'I want to be tested on cross-border structuring, on pricing against a global book, on working with LPs or institutional investors who have a different risk language.' That signals intellectual honesty and drive — two things foreign bank MDs value over a polished but hollow pitch.

The illustrative sketch: 'On Bajaj Housing Finance, I was inside the regulatory dialogue as the RBI's NHB framework was shifting in real time. A foreign co-manager couldn't walk in and read that room the way we could. That contextual intelligence is what I bring. What I don't yet have is the discipline of a globally distributed bookbuild — that's exactly why I'm here.'

Short, honest, forward-leaning. That's the frame.
